title: 第 26 節
en: On the day on which the corpse had been burnt, the relatives (samanodakas) bathed and poured out a handful of water to the deceased, pronouncing his name and that of his family.t At sunset they returned home, and, as was but natural, they were told to cook nothing during the first night, and to observe certain rules during the next day up to ten days* according to the character of the deceased. These were days of mourning, or, as they were afterwards called, days of impurity, when the mourners withdrew from contact with the world, and shrank by a schrift der Deutschen Morgenlandischen Gesellschaft, vol. ix, 1856, t Ajvalayana Gr/hya-siitras IV. 4, 10.

^4^ WHAT CAN INDIA TEACH li^ I natural impulse from the ordinary occupations and pleasures of life.^ Then followed the collecting of the ashes on the nth, 13th or 15th day of the dark half of the moon. On returning from thence they bathed, and then offered what was called a ^raddha to the departed.

This word 6'raddha, which meets us here for the first time, is full of interesting lessons, if only properly understood. First of all it should be noted that it is absent, not only from the hymns, but, so far as we know at present, even from the ancient Brahma/zas. It seems therefore a word of a more modern origin. There is a passage in Apastamba's Dharma-sutras which betrays, on the part of the author, a consciousness of the more modern origin of the .Sraddhas : — f " Formerly men and gods lived together in this world. Then the gods in reward of their sacrifices went to heaven, but men were left behind. Those men who perform sacrifices in the same manner as the gods did, dwelt (after death)*with the gods and Brahman in heaven. Now (seeing men left behind) Manu revealed this ceremony which is designated by the word Sraddha."

Sraddha has assumed many | meanings, and Manu,§ t Biihler, Apastamba, Sacred Books of the East, vol. ii. p. 138 ; also ^raddh^kalpa, p. 890. Though the 6'raddha is prescribed in the Gobhiliya Grz'hya-sutras, IV . 4, 2-3, it is not described there, but in a separate treatise, the ^Sraddha-kalpa.

I As meaning the food, jraddha occurs in JiMdhabhu^ and similar words. As meaning the sacrificial act, it is explained, yatraitai ^/^raddhaya diyate tad eva karma jraddhaj-abdabhidheyam. Pretam pitrims kz. nirdii-ya bho^yaz;^ yat priyam atmana/^ jraddhaya diyate yatra tai/^^r§,ddhamparikirtitam. Gobhiliya Gr/hya-sfitras, p. 892. We also read j-raddh^nvita/z jraddhaw kurvita, "let a man perform the jraddha with faith j " Gobhiliya Gr^hya-sutras, p. 1053.
§ Manu III, 82.

VEDA AND VEDANTA. 247 for instance, uses it almost synonymously with pit? /y2ign3.. B'ut its original meaning seems to have been " that which is given with iraddha or faith," i.e. charity bestowed on deserving persons, and, more particularly, on Brahma;zas. The gift was called sraddha, but the act itself also was called by the same name. The word is best explained by Naraya;^a in his commentary on the Gnhya-sutras of Asvalayana (IV. 7), " *Sraddha is that which is given in faith to Brahmans for the sake" of the Fathers." * Such charitable gifts flowed most naturally and abundantly at the time of a man's death, or whenever his memory was revived by happy or unhappy events in a family, and hence Sraddha has become the general name for ever so many sacred acts commemorative of the departed. We hear of 5raddhas not only at funerals, but at joyous events also, when presents were bestowed in the name of the family, and therefore in the name of the ancestors also, on all who had a right to that distinction.

It is a mistake therefore to look upon »Sraddhas simply as offerings of water or cakes to the Fathers. An offering to the Fathers was, no doubt, a symbolic part of each *Sraddha, but its more important character was charity bestowed in memory of the Fathers.

This, in time, gave rise to much abuse, like the alms bestowed on the Church during the Middle Ages, But in the beginning the motive was excellent. It was simply a wish to benefit others, arising from the conviction, felt more strongly in the presence of death than at any other time, that as we can carry 248 WHAT CAN INDIA TEACH us f nothing out of this world, we ought to do as much good as possible in the world with our worldly goods. At *Sraddhas the Brahma?/as were said to represent the sacrificial fire into which the gifts should be thrown.* If we translate here Brahma/^as by priests, we can easily understand why there should have been in later times so strong a feeling against 5raddhas. But priest is a very bad rendering of Brahma;2a. The Brahma;/as were, socially and intellectually, a class of men of high breeding. They were a recognised and, no doubt, a most essential element in the ancient society of India. As they lived for others, and were excluded from most of the lucrative pursuits of life, it was a social, and it soon became a religious duty, that they should be supported by the community at large. Great care was taken that the recipients of such bounty as was bestowed at .Sraddhas should be strangers, neither friends nor enemies, and in no way related to the family. Thus Apastamba says :f " The food eaten ( at a ^Sraddha ) by persons related to the giver is a gift offered to gob_ lins. It reaches neither the Manes nor the Gods.'' A man who tried to curry favor by bestowing ^Sraddhika gifts, was called by an opprobrious name, a iSraddha-mitra. % Without denying therefore that in later times the system of *Sraddhas may have degenerated, I think we can perceive that it sprang from a pure source^ and, what for our present purpose is even more important, from an intelligible source.

Let us now return to the passage in the Grihya- t L, c. p. 142, I Manu III. 138, 140, VEDA AND VEDAMTA. 249 stjtras of Asavalyana, where we met for the first time with the name of 5raddha. * It was the >Sraddha to be given for the sake of the Departed, after his ashes had been collected in an urn and buried, This xSraddha is called ekoddishta, f or, as we should say, personal. It was meant for one person only, not for the three ancestors, nor for all the ancestors. Its object was in fact to raise the departed to the rank of a Pitri, and this had to be achieved by ^raddha offerings continued during a whole year. This at least is the general, and, most likely, the original rule. Apastamba says that the ^raddha for a deceased relative should be performed every day during the year, and that after that a monthly ^Sraddha only should be performed or none at all, that is, no more personal 5raddha, % because the departed shares henceforth in the regular Prava;^a-i-raddhas. § Sankayana says the same, || namely that the personal ^raddhalasts for a year, and that then " the Fourth" is dropped, i. e. the great-grandfather was dropped, the grandfather became the great-grandfather, the father the grandfather, while the lately Departed occupied the^father's place among the three principal Pitris. ^ This was § It is discribed as a vSkri^x of the Parvawa-jraddha in Gobhiliya Grihya-sutras, p. loii.

X One of the differences between the acts before and after the Sapi«^ikara«a is noted by Salankayana : — Sapi;zi/ikara«am y§.vad rz^udarbhai/^ pitrikriya Sapi«^ikara«ad ardhvawz divigu«air vidhivad bhavet. Gobhiliya Gr/hya-sutras, p, 930.
§ Gobhiliya Gr/hya-sutras, p. 1023.
II G?'/hya-sntras, ed. Oldenberg, p. Z'^.
^ A pratyabdikam ekoddish/am on the annivtrsary of the deceased is mentienad by Gobhiliya, I. c. p. loii.
250 ^f^A T CAN INDIA TMACB US f called the Sapi«^ikara;^a, i. e. the elevating of the departed to the rank of an ancestor.

There are here, as elsewhere, many exceptions. Gobhiliya allows six months instead of a year, or even a Tripaksha,* i. e. three half-months; and lastly, any auspicious event (vriddhi) may become the occasion of the Sapi;^</Jkara;2a.t '

The full number of 5raddhas necessary for the Sapindana is sometimes given as sixteen, viz. the first, then one in each of the twelve months, then two semestral ones, and lastly the Sapindana. But here too much variety is allowed, though, if the Sapindana takes place before the end of the year, the number of sixteen 5raddhas has still to be made up.J When the ^raddha is offered on account of an auspicious event, such as a birth or a marriage, the fathers invoked are not the father, grandfather, and great-grandfather, who are sometimes called ai'rumukha, with tearful faces, but the ancestors before them, and they are called nandimukha, or joyful.§ Colebrooke,|| to whom we owe an excellent description of what a ^Sraddha is in tnodern times, took evidently the same view. "The first set of funeral ceremonies," he writes, " is adapted to effect, by means of oblations, the re-imbodying of the soul of the deceased, after burning his corpse. The apparent scope of the second set is to raise his shade t -5'ankh. G^-zhya, p. 83; Gobh. GrzTiya, p. 1024. According to some authorities the ekoddish^fa is called nava, new, during ten days ; navamj-ra, mixed, for six months ; and pura«a, old, afterwards. Gobhiliya Gr/hya-sutras, p. 1020.
} Gobhiliya, c. p. 1032.
§ Gobhiliya. 1. c, p. 1047. li Life and Essays, ii, p. 195.

from this world, where it would else, according to the notions of the Hindus, continue to roam among demons and evil spirits, up to heaven, and then deify him, as it were, among the manes of departed ancestors. For this end, a ^raddha should regularly be offered to the deceased on the day after the mourning expires ; twelve other 6'raddhas singly to the deceased in twelve successive months ; similar obsequies at the end of the third fortnight, and also in the sixth month, and in the twelfth ; and the oblation called Sapi72^ana on the first anniversary of his decease.* At this Sapi;/<^ana ^Sraddha, which is the last of the ekoddish/a j-raddha, four funeral cakes are offered to the deceased and his three ancestors, that consecrated to the deceased being divided into three portions and mixed with the other three cakes.
The portion retained is often offered to the deceased, and the act of union and fellowship becomes complete."!

When this system of .Sraddha, had once been started, it seems to have spread very rapidly. We soon hear of the monthly »Sraddha, not only in memory of one person lately deceased, but as part of the Pitr/ya^;1a, and as obligatory, not only on householders (agnimat), but on other persons also, and, not only on the three upper castes, but even, teen ceremonies, and for the concluding obsequies entitled Sapi«i/ana, are anticipated, and the whole is completed on the second- t)r third day ; after which they are again performed at the proper times, but in honor of the whole set of progenitors instead of the deceased singly. It is this which Dr. Donner, in his learned paper on the Piw^apitrvj2ighz. (p. ii), takes as the general rule.
t See this subject most exhaustively treated, particularly in its bearings on the law of inheritance, in Rajkumar Sarvadahikri's Tagore Law Lectures for 1880, p. 93.

2 5 2 ^^^ T CAN INDIA TEACH US f without hymns, on Sf^dras,* and as to be performed, not only on the day of New-Moon, but on other days also, t whenever there was an opportunity. Gobhila seems to look upon the Pi?2<^apitr/a^;1a as itself a 5raddha, % and the commentator holds that, even if there are no pi?/(/as or cakes, the Brahmans ought still to be fed. This ^Sraddha, however, is distinguished from the other, the true -5'raddha, called Anvaharya, which follows it, § and which is properly known by the name of Parva;2a .Sraddha.

The same difficulties which confront us when we try to form a clear conception of the character of the various ancestral ceremonies, were felt by the Brahmans themselves, as may be seen from the long discussions in the commentary on the 5raddha-kalpa || and from the abusive language used by iTandrakanta Tarkalankara against Raghunandana. The question with them assumes the form of what is pradhana (primary) and what is anga (secondary) in these sacrifices, and the final result arrived at is that sometimes the offering of cakes is pradhana, as in the Tinda-pitriya^fia., sometimes the feeding of Brahas only, as in the Nitya-jraddha, sometimes both, as in the Sapi;/^ikara/za.
We may safely say, therefore, that not a day passed J See p. 666, and p. 1008. Grzhyaktrak piw^apitr/ya^lasya jraddhatvam aha.

§ Gobhiliya IV. 4, 3, itarad anvah§,ryam. But the commentators add, anagner amavasya^raddham, nanvaharyam. According to Gobhiliya there ought to be the Vaixvadeva offering and the Bali offering at the end of each Pitrvawa-jraddha ; see Gobhiliya Gr/hyasfitras, p. 1005, but no Vaixvadeva at an ckoddish/a jT^ddha, 1. c. p.
Ii L. c. pp. 1005-IGIO ; Nimaayasindhu, p. 27*.

in the life of the ancient people of India on which they were not reminded of their ancestors, both near and distant, and showed their respect for them, partly by symbolic offerings to their Manes, partly by charitable gifts to deserving persons, chiefly Brahmans. These offertories varied from the simplest, such as milk and fruits, to the costliest, such as gold and jewels. The feasts given to those who were invited to officiate or assist at a ^Yaddha seem in some cases to have been very sumptuous, * and what is very important, the eating of meat, which in later times was strictly forbidden in many sects, must, when the Sutras were written, have been fully recognized at these feasts, even to the killing and eating of a cow.t This shows that these .Sraddhas, though possibly of later date than the Pitnya^as, belong nevertheless to a very early phase of Indian life. And though much may have been changed in the outward form of these ancient ancestral sacrifices, their original solemn character has remained unchanged. Even at present, when the worship of the ancient Devas is ridiculed by many who still take part in it, the worship of the ancestors and the offering of *Sraddbas have maintained much of their old sacred character. They have sometimes been compared to the " communion" in the Christian Church, and it is certainly true that many natives speak of their funeral and ancestral ceremonies with a hushed voice and with real reverence. They alone seem still to impart to their •^ Kalau tavad gavalamblio mS7?;sadanaz« kdi jr^ddhe nishiddleham, Gobhilena tu madhyamash^akayaw? vflstukarmawi ia gavalatnbho vihita/z, m&wsaiarus /C-anvash/akyajraddlie ; Gobhiliya Grihya-sutra, cd, i^andrakS.nta Tarkalankara, Vi^apati, p. 8, 254 ^^^ ^ ^^^ INDIA TEACH US^ life on earth a deeper significance and a higher prospect. I could go even a step further and express my belief, that the absence of such services for the dead and of ancestral commemorations is a real loss in our own religion. Almost every religion recognizes them as tokens of a loving memory offered to a father, to a mother, or even to a child, and though in many countries they may have proved a source of superstition, there runs through them all a deep well of living human faith that ought never to be allowed to perish. The early Christian Church had to sanction the ancient prayers for the Souls of the departed, and in more Southern countries the services on All Saints' and on All Souls' Day continue to satisfy a craving of the human heart which must be satisfied in every religion. We, in the North, shrink from these open manifestations of grief, but our hearts know often a deeper bitterness ; nay, there would seem to be a higher truth than we at first imagine in the belief of the ancients that the souls of our beloved ones leave us no rest, unless they are appeased by daily prayers, or better still, by daily acts of goodness in remembrance of them.* But there is still another Beyond that found expression in the ancient religion of India. Besides the Devas or Gods, and besides the Pitr/s or Fathers, there was a third world, without which the ancient religion of India could not have become what we see it in the Veda. That third Beyond was what the poets of the Veda call the Ritd^, and which I believe meant originally no more than " the straight line." It is applied to the straight line of the sun in its VEDA AND VEDANTA. 255 daily course, to the straight line followed by day and night, to the straight line that regulates the seasons, to the straight line which, in spite of many momentary deviations, was discovered to run through the whole realm of nature. We call that i?/ta, that straight, direct, or right line, when we apply it in a more general sense, the Law of Nature ; and when we apply it to the moral world, we try to express the same idea again by speaking of the Moral Law, the law on which our life is founded, the eternal Law of Right and Reason, or, it may be, " that which makes for righteousness '' both within us and without. * And thus, as a thoughtful look on nature led to the first perception of bright gods, and in the end of a God of light, as love of our parents was transfigured into piety and a belief in immortality, a recognition of the straight lines in the world without, and in the world within, was raised into the highest faith, a faith in a law that underlies everything, a law in which we may trust, whatever befall, a law which speaks within us with the divine voice of conscience, and tells us "■ this is r/ta," "this is right," " this is true," whatever the statutes of our ancestors, or even the voices of our bright gods, may say to the contrary.

These three Beyonds are the three revelations of antiquity ; and it is due almost entirely to the discovery of the Veda that we, in this nineteenth century of ours, have been allowed to watch again these early phases of thought and religion, which had passed away long before the beginnings of other literatures.!
zh: 屍體火化的當日，親屬（samanodakas）沐浴並向亡者澆灑一把水，誦念其名字及其家族名號。日落時分，他們返回家中；自然而然地，他們被告知在第一個夜晚不得烹飪任何食物，並在接下來的日子裡遵守某些規則，期間長達十天*，具體時間視亡者的身分而定。這些乃是居喪的日子，或如後來所稱的「不淨」（impurity）之日，在此期間居喪者與世俗隔離，並迴避一切接觸。t --- t 《阿什瓦拉亞那家庭經》（Ajvalayana Gṛhya-sūtras） IV. 4, 10.
^4^ 印度能教導我們什麼呢？^I 人們被一股自然的衝動引離日常的職業與生活樂趣。^隨後在月圓缺期間的第九、第十三或第十五天收集骨灰。從那裡返回後，他們沐浴，然後向亡者獻上所謂的*raddha*（薦亡祭）。

這個在此首次出現的詞彙「信心」（*Sraddha*），如果妥善理解，便蘊含著許多有趣的教訓。首先應當注意到，它不僅缺席於讚歌之中，而且據我們目前所知，甚至也不見於古老的《梵書》（Brahmanas）。因此它似乎是一個較為現代的詞彙。在阿帕斯坦巴的《法經》（Apastamba's Dharma-sutras）中有一段話，足以顯示作者對於《信心祭》（Sraddhas）較為現代起源的認識：「從前人類與諸神曾在此世界中共同生活。其後諸神因其祭祀而上升至天界，然而人類被遺留在後。那些以諸神當初進行祭祀的相同方式而進行祭祀的人類，死後與諸神及梵天共居於天界。現今（見人類被遺留在後），摩奴啟示了這一儀式，乃被指稱為『信心』（*Sraddha*）之名。」
Sraddha 已經演變出許多含義，而 Manu§、†Bühler、Apastamba《東方聖書》第二卷第 138 頁；另見《Sraddha-kalpa》第 890 頁。雖然《Gobhiliya Grhya-sutras》IV. 4, 2-3 中規定了 Sraddha，但並未在該處詳細說明，而是在單獨的論著《Sraddha-kalpa》中論述。

我認為「信」（śraddha）一字作為「食物」之意時，出現在《吉曼陀婆》（Jimdhabhu）及類似詞彙中。作為「祭祀行為」之意時，經文解釋為：「凡以信心獻祭者，此即名為以信心（śraddha）所指稱之業」。祭祀亡者及祖先時，「以信心獻上所摯愛者，即謂為信心（śraddha）所稱頌之處」（《高毗梨耶居家經》，第892頁）。我們亦見到「以信心執行信祭」（śraddhānvitaḥ śraddhaṃ kurvita）的表述，意為「願人以信心執行信祭」（《高毗梨耶居家經》，第1053頁）。
§ 摩奴法典第三卷，第82節

247 例如，它幾乎與 pit?y2ign3 同義使用。但其原始含義似乎是「以信心（iraddha）或信仰而給予之物」，即施予於應受之人，尤其是施予於婆羅門。此禮物被稱為 sraddha，但此行為本身也用同一名稱稱之。阿斯瓦拉亞那《家庭經》（Gṛhya-sūtra）的註釋者那羅延（Nārāyaṇa）最善於解釋此詞（IV. 7）：「*Sraddha 是為父親之故，以信心施予婆羅門之物。*」此類慈善施與最自然、最豐富地發生於人死亡時，或家族中幸福或不幸之事喚起其記憶時，因此 Sraddha 已成為眾多紀念亡者之聖儀的總稱。我們不僅聽聞 Sraddha 出現於葬禮，亦出現於歡樂事件，當時禮物以家族名義贈予，因此亦以祖先名義贈予所有有權獲此榮譽之人。
因此，將「施捨祭」（Sraddhas）單純視為獻給祖先的水或糕餅祭祀，乃是一種誤解。向祖先獻祭無疑是每場施捨祭的象徵性部分，但其更為重要的特質是為了紀念祖先而進行的慈善行為。

這種做法日久天長便導致許多弊端，如同中世紀教會所接受的施捨一樣。但在最初，動機是純淨的。它不過是出於一種願望——希望造福他人，而這種信念在面對死亡時比其他任何時刻都更加深切：既然我們無法將任何東西帶出這個世界，就應當用我們在世間的財富盡可能地行善。在*娑羅陀*(Sraddhas)儀式中，婆羅門*(Brahmas)*據說代表犧牲的聖火，供物應當投入其中。如果我們在此將婆羅門譯為祭司，就不難理解為什麼後世對娑羅陀儀式會有如此強烈的反感。但祭司對婆羅門的翻譯實在不當。婆羅門在社會地位和智識上都是高貴出身的一個階級。他們是古代印度社會中公認的、無疑也是最為必要的一個成分。由於他們為他人而活，並被排除在人生中大多數有利可圖的職業之外，社會——並隨後成為宗教——的責任就是由整個社群來供養他們。人們非常謹慎地確保在娑羅陀儀式中所受施捨的對象應當是陌生人，既非朋友亦非敵人，與家族毫無關係。因此阿帕斯坦巴*(Apastamba)*說：「由與施者有關之人所食用的（娑羅陀儀式中的）食物，乃是獻給鬼魅的祭品。它既不能到達祖靈，也不能到達眾神。」一個企圖透過賜予娑羅陀*(Sraddhika)*禮物來討好他人的人，被稱以貶低的名號——娑羅陀友人*(Sraddhа-mitra)*。因此，雖然我不否認娑羅陀體系在後代可能已經衰變，但我認為我們可以看出它起源於純淨的源頭，而且對於我們目前的目的而言，更為重要的是，它起源於一個可理解的源頭。

現在讓我們回到《阿沙瓦拉亞那住居經》（Grihya-sutras of Asavalyana）的那段文獻，那是我們首次遇見「施食」（śraddha）這一名稱的地方。這份施食是為亡者而舉行的，在其骨灰被收集到骨灰甕中並埋葬之後。這種施食被稱為「單一指向施食」（ekoddishta），或者按我們的說法，是個人性的施食。它僅針對一個人，而非三位祖先，也非所有祖先。其目的實際上是將亡者提升至毘多羅（Pitri）的地位，而這必須透過持續整整一年的施食祭祀才能達成。這至少是普遍的，而且很可能是最初的規則。阿帕斯坦巴（Apastamba）說，對已故親屬的施食應該在那一年中每日舉行，在此之後則應只舉行月度施食或根本不舉行，也就是說，不再進行個人性的施食，因為亡者自此之後便參與定期的普拉瓦亞施食（Pravaya-śraddha）。尚卡亞那（Sankayana）也這樣說，即個人性的施食持續一年，其後則「第四位」被捨棄，也就是說，曾祖父被捨棄，祖父遂成為曾祖父，父親成為祖父，而新近的亡者則占據三位主要毘多羅中父親的位置。這是在《果畢利亞住居經》（Gobhiliya Grihya-sutras）中被記述為普拉瓦亞施食（Parvaya-śraddha）的一種變式。
X 薩蘭卡亞那指出，明火灌禮前後的行為差異之一如下：——明火灌禮前，應如喪禮般進行；明火灌禮後，應如天界儀式般如法進行。（*Sapindikaranam yavad rudharbbai pitrikriya Sapindikarad ardhvam divigunam vidhivad bhavet*）。《葛毗利耶家庭經》（Gobhiliya Grhya-sutras），第九三〇頁。
§ 《高不黎耶家庭經》（Gobhiliya Gr/hya-sutras），第1023頁。
II《居家經》(Gṛhya-sūtras)，Oldenberg編本，第Z頁。
^ 戈婆利亞（Gobhiliya）在其著作第一卷第一百二十頁提及，在亡者忌日所進行的週年祭祀（pratyabdikam ekoddish/am）。
可以稱之為 Sapindīkaraṇa，即將亡者提升至祖先地位的儀式。
這裡如同其他地方一樣，存在許多例外。高毗利耶（Gobhiliya）允許以六個月代替一年，甚至允許三半月期（Tripaksha）*；最後，任何吉祥的事件（vriddhi）都可能成為薩皮；卡羅提亞的機會。²

薩品達納（Sapindana）所需之全部施身祭（Sraddha）的數目，有時被認為是十六次，即第一次，其後每月各一次（共十二個月），然後是兩次半年性的施身祭，最後是薩品達納。但此處允許很大的變異，不過若薩品達納在年終前舉行，十六次施身祭的數目仍須補足。當施身祭因誕生或婚禮等吉祥事件而舉行時，所祭祀的先祖並非父親、祖父和曾祖父（他們有時被稱為「淚面」[*ai'rumukha*]），而是更早的祖先，他們被稱為「喜面」[*nandimukha*]。我們得益於科爾布魯克（Colebrooke）對現代施身祭的詳盡描述，他顯然持相同的看法。他寫道：「第一套喪葬儀式的目的，是藉由奠祭之法，在焚燒亡者遺體後，使其靈魂得以重新入體。第二套儀式的明顯目的，則是提升其陰靈。」 --- *註*：G^-zhya, p. 83; Gobh. Gr̥hya, p. 1024. 根據某些權威的說法，單一亡靈祭（ekoddish^fa）在十日內稱為「新」（*nava*）；六個月內稱為「混合」（*navamj-ra*）；之後稱為「舊」（*pura«a*）。Gobhiliya Gr̥hya-sutras, p. 1020.
} 高比利亞（Gobhiliya），頁 1032。
§ 高比利亞（Gobhiliya）。第1冊，第1047頁。二《生平與論文集》第二卷，第195頁。

從這個世界救出亡者，否則按照印度人的觀念，亡者會繼續在魔鬼與惡靈之間遊蕩；將亡者送上天堂，並且如同在已逝祖先的英靈中將其神化。為此目的，應在服喪期滿後的第二天向亡者獻上一次 *śraddha*；在隨後十二個月內分別向亡者獻上十二次 *śraddha*；在第三個十五天後、第六個月以及第十二個月時進行類似的祭禮；並在其忌辰的第一週年時獻上稱為 *Sapinḍana* 的祭品。在此 *Sapinḍana śraddha*（即最後一次的 *ekoddisha śraddha*）中，向亡者及其三位祖先各獻上一塊喪禮餅餅，其中獻給亡者的那塊分成三份，與其他三塊餅餅混合在一起。
保留下來的部分常被獻給亡者，如此一來結合與交誼的行為就臻於完美。」

當這套薩剌陀（Śraddha）制度一旦確立，似乎就迅速地廣泛傳播開來。我們很快就聽聞到月薩剌陀（monthly Śraddha）的做法，不僅是為最近去世的一個人的追思，而且成為祖靈祭祀（Pitṛyajña）的一部分，並且不僅對住居者（agnimat）具有約束力，對其他人也是如此；並非只限於三個上層種姓，甚至在青年儀式中，以及稱為沙皮尼卡拉那（Sapindi/kara）的最終喪禮儀式中，這些都被提前進行，整個過程在第二或第三天內完成；之後它們會在適當的時間再次舉行，但此時是為整個祖先的序列舉行，而非單獨為亡者舉行。這就是唐納博士（Dr. Donner）在其關於祖靈供養（Piw^apitrvj2ighz）的學術論文（第二頁）中所視為普遍規則的做法。
見拉吉庫瑪爾‧薩瓦達希克里（Rajkumar Sarvadahikri）一八八○年泰戈爾法律講座（Tagore Law Lectures）之第九十三頁，該書對此主題進行了最詳盡的論述，特別是在其對繼承法之關係上的闡發。

2 5 2 ^^^ 印度能教導我們什麼 沒有讚歌，論及首陀羅，*並且應該舉行的方式不只在新月日，也在其他日子裡，† 無論何時有機會。高畢拉似乎將皮?2<^阿彼特瑞雅達^;1a 視為本身就是一種薩拉達（śraddha），%而註釋家認為，即使沒有皮?/(/阿斯（piṇḍas）或糕餅，婆羅門仍應受款待。然而這種薩拉達與其他的區別開來，即真正的薩拉達，稱為隨後施食（Anvaharya），§ 並由帕爾瓦薩拉達（Parva-śraddha）之名恰當地為人所知。

當我們試圖對各種祖先祭儀的性質形成清晰的認識時，我們所面臨的同樣困難，也曾為婆羅門人自己所感受到。這一點可從《斯拉提‧卡爾帕》（śraddha-kalpa）的注疏中的冗長討論，以及譚德拉坎塔‧塔卡蘭卡拉（Tandrakanta Tarkalankara）對拉古南達納（Raghunandana）所使用的咒罵言辭中看出。對他們而言，這個問題呈現為在這些祭祀中何者是普拉達那（pradhana，本質的）、何者是安迦（anga，附屬的）的形式，而最終所達成的結論是：有時餅餤的奉獻是普拉達那，如同在《廷達‧披特里亞祭》（Tinda-pitriya-yajña）中那樣；有時只有婆羅門人的供食才是普拉達那，如同在《尼提亞‧斯拉提》（Nitya-śraddha）中那樣；有時兩者兼備，如同在《薩皮‧尼德卡拉納》（Sapiṇḍīkaraṇa）中那樣。
因此，我們可以放心地說，沒有一天經過J 見第 666 頁和第 1008 頁。Grzhyaktrak piw^apitr/ya^lasya jraddhatvam aha.

§ Gobhiliya IV. 4, 3, itarad anvah§,ryam。但註釋家補充說，anagner amavasya^raddham, nanvaharyam。根據 Gobhiliya 的說法，每次 Pitrvawa-jraddha 的末尾應當有毗濕瓦提婆祭祀（Vaixvadeva offering）與巴利祭祀（Bali offering）；參見 Gobhiliya Gr/hyasfitras，頁 1005，但在 ckoddish/a jT^ddha 中無毗濕瓦提婆祭祀，同上頁。
II 《同上》，頁 1005-1010；《尼馬雅辛杜》（Nimaayasindhu），第 27 頁。

在古代印度人民的生活中，幾乎沒有任何時刻不被提醒他們的祖先——無論遠祖還是近親——因此他們表示對祖先的尊敬，一部分是透過對其亡靈（Manes）的象徵性獻祭，一部分是透過對值得尊敬者——特別是婆羅門的慈善贈禮。這些供物從最簡樸的——如牛奶和水果——到最珍貴的——如黃金和珠寶——應有盡有。在祭祀（Yaddha）中被邀請主持或協助的人享受的盛宴，在某些情況下似乎極為豐盛；而最為重要的是，肉食在後世許多教派中受到嚴格禁止，但在《經書》（Sutras）成書時，必定在這些盛宴中得到充分認可，甚至包括宰殺與食用牛隻。這表明這些《薦亡祭》（Sraddhas）雖然可能晚於《祖靈祭》（Pitryas），然而仍然屬於印度生活的極早階段。儘管這些古老祖先祭祀的外在形式可能已有許多改變，其原初的莊嚴性質卻保持不變。即使在當今，雖然許多仍參與其中的人嘲笑對古代提婆的崇拜，但對祖先的崇拜和《薦亡祭》的獻祭仍維持著許多古老的聖聖性。它們有時被比擬為基督教堂中的「聖餐」，而且確實許多信徒用低沉的聲音、帶著真摯的虔敬談論他們的喪葬與祖先儀式。它們似乎是唯一仍能賦予塵世生活更深刻的意義與更遠大前景的。我可以進一步表達我的信念：在我們自己的宗教中，缺乏對亡者的紀念服務與祖先追悼是一種真實的喪失。幾乎每一宗教都承認這些是對父親、母親，甚至對孩子的愛心記憶的標記，雖然在許多國家它們可能成為迷信的根源，但貫穿其中始終有一股深沉的生活人性信仰之泉，不應被允許枯竭。早期基督教會不得不認可對亡靈的古老祈禱，而在更多南方國家，在諸聖節和追思亡者節舉辦的儀式繼續滿足人心中必然要被滿足的渴望。我們北方人對這些公開的悲傷表現感到退縮，但我們的心常知道更深刻的悲苦；實際上，古人的信念中似乎存在著比我們初時想像更高的真理——我們摯愛的靈魂如不受到日常祈禱或更好地日常善行的安撫，便不會給我們片刻安寧。 但古代印度宗教還表達了另一個「彼岸」。除了提婆或眾神、除了毗多羅或祖先之外，還有第三個世界，沒有它，古代印度宗教就不會成為我們在《吠陀》中所見的樣子。那第三個彼岸就是《吠陀》詩人所稱的《律陀》（Ritá），我認為其原初意義不過是「直線」。它應用於太陽在其日常運行中的直線，應用於晝夜所循循的直線，應用於規律四時的直線，應用於那條直線——儘管有許多瞬間的偏差，卻被發現貫穿整個自然界。當我們以更一般的意義應用時，我們稱那律陀、那直線、直接的或正確的直線為「自然律」；而當我們應用於道德世界時，我們試圖再次用講述「道德律」來表達同一觀念——我們生命所基於的律法、永恆的正義與理性之律，或者或許是「那促進正義的者」，無論是在我們內在還是外在。 因此，正如對自然的深思觀照導致對光輝眾神的首次感知，最終導致對光神的信仰，正如對父母的愛被轉化為虔敬與對不朽的信念，對外在世界與內在世界中直線的認識被提升為最高信仰——一種對貫穿一切的律法的信仰、一種我們可以信賴的律法，無論發生何事，一種以良心的神聖聲音在我們心中說話的律法，告訴我們「這是律陀」、「這是正確的」、「這是真實的」，無論我們祖先的成文法或甚至我們光輝眾神的聲音可能如何相反。
這三個「彼岸」乃是遠古時代的三重啟示；幾乎完全因為吠陀的發現，我們在此十九世紀才得以重新觀察這些思想與宗教的早期階段，而這些階段早已在其他文獻的起源之前就消逝無蹤了。
